APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work sits squarely within the NLP/ML remit: clustering documents using word-embedding representations and Wasserstein distances is topical and of clear interest to both communities. The problem formulation (discrete distributions over embedding supports and D2‑clustering with barycenters) and the breadth of empirical comparisons (multiple standard corpora, many baselines, AMI/ARI and related metrics) align with the evaluation standards and audience expectations of top venues. Code release and thorough baseline coverage further strengthen its suitability for this community. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it advances document clustering by combining word embeddings with Wasserstein-distance D2-clustering and provides algorithmic and engineering contributions (modified B-ADMM, Elkan pruning, parallelization) that address practical scalability. Evaluation uses standard datasets (20Newsgroups, Reuters, BBC, Ohsumed), a comprehensive set of baselines (tf-idf, PV, LDA, NMF, Kusner et al.), and accepted clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure), matching venue expectations. Releasing code and reporting hyperparameters further strengthens reproducibility and suitability for the community. From an applied-NLP engineering perspective the method’s empirical robustness across document lengths and provided implementation details make it an excellent fit for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely within the NLP/ML remit: clustering documents using word-embedding representations and Wasserstein distances is topical and of clear interest to both communities. The problem formulation (discrete distributions over embedding supports and D2‑clustering with barycenters) and the breadth of empirical comparisons (multiple standard corpora, many baselines, AMI/ARI and related metrics) align with the evaluation standards and audience expectations of top venues. Code release and thorough baseline coverage further strengthen its suitability for this community.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it advances document clustering by combining word embeddings with Wasserstein-distance D2-clustering and provides algorithmic and engineering contributions (modified B-ADMM, Elkan pruning, parallelization) that address practical scalability. Evaluation uses standard datasets (20Newsgroups, Reuters, BBC, Ohsumed), a comprehensive set of baselines (tf-idf, PV, LDA, NMF, Kusner et al.), and accepted clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure), matching venue expectations. Releasing code and reporting hyperparameters further strengthens reproducibility and suitability for the community. From an applied-NLP engineering perspective the method’s empirical robustness across document lengths and provided implementation details make it an excellent fit for a top conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to method (D2-clustering with Wasserstein distance) and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (modified B-ADMM, parallelization/Elkan pruning, and empirical gains). Notation and definitions are presented consistently for the discrete-distribution formulation and barycenter computation, and figures/tables effectively illustrate comparative performance across datasets (BBCNews, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, Ohsumed). Writing is generally clear and grammatically sound, though a few sections could benefit from tighter signposting of algorithmic steps and complexity summaries to aid readers focused on theoretical guarantees. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and states its main contributions early (D2‑clustering with Wasserstein distance, algorithmic/parallelization improvements, and empirical gains). Notation and methodological descriptions are mostly consistent and the writing quality is reported as good; experiments are presented with many datasets, metrics, and hyperparameter details which suggests effective figures/tables and examples. My only reservations are minor presentation density around the modified B‑ADMM and pruning tricks where readers may need to read carefully to reproduce implementation details, but overall clarity is strong.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to method (D2-clustering with Wasserstein distance) and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (modified B-ADMM, parallelization/Elkan pruning, and empirical gains). Notation and definitions are presented consistently for the discrete-distribution formulation and barycenter computation, and figures/tables effectively illustrate comparative performance across datasets (BBCNews, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, Ohsumed). Writing is generally clear and grammatically sound, though a few sections could benefit from tighter signposting of algorithmic steps and complexity summaries to aid readers focused on theoretical guarantees.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and states its main contributions early (D2‑clustering with Wasserstein distance, algorithmic/parallelization improvements, and empirical gains). Notation and methodological descriptions are mostly consistent and the writing quality is reported as good; experiments are presented with many datasets, metrics, and hyperparameter details which suggests effective figures/tables and examples. My only reservations are minor presentation density around the modified B‑ADMM and pruning tricks where readers may need to read carefully to reproduce implementation details, but overall clarity is strong.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (tf-idf variants, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, LSI, spectral/LPP, and Kusner et al. 2015) across multiple standard datasets and uses common cluster metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure, etc.), with hyperparameters reported and code available—supporting largely fair comparisons. It also discusses computational tradeoffs and limitations (e.g., Ohsumed results and Kusner’s scalability), and describes algorithmic differences (modified B-ADMM, Elkan pruning). As a theory-focused reviewer I note a minor shortcoming: the summary does not indicate careful acknowledgment or per-dataset analysis where prior methods might match or beat the proposed approach (beyond a brief Ohsumed remark), nor mention of statistical significance/variance reporting, so the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive in addressing when baselines perform comparably. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (tf-idf variants, AvgDoc, Paragraph Vectors, LDA, NMF, LSI, spectral/LPP, and the relevant Kusner et al. 2015 KNN-graph OT method) across multiple standard datasets and uses consistent clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure, etc.). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics, hyperparameters reported, code released) and practical scalability concerns of competing methods are discussed. However, the summary does not indicate inclusion of some more recent embedding-based or OT-based competitors (e.g., transformer-derived document embeddings or newer scalable OT approximations), and it is only partially clear whether cases where baselines match or beat the method are fully acknowledged and analyzed, so I deduct one point.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (tf-idf variants, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, LSI, spectral/LPP, and Kusner et al. 2015) across multiple standard datasets and uses common cluster metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure, etc.), with hyperparameters reported and code available—supporting largely fair comparisons. It also discusses computational tradeoffs and limitations (e.g., Ohsumed results and Kusner’s scalability), and describes algorithmic differences (modified B-ADMM, Elkan pruning). As a theory-focused reviewer I note a minor shortcoming: the summary does not indicate careful acknowledgment or per-dataset analysis where prior methods might match or beat the proposed approach (beyond a brief Ohsumed remark), nor mention of statistical significance/variance reporting, so the comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive in addressing when baselines perform comparably.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (tf-idf variants, AvgDoc, Paragraph Vectors, LDA, NMF, LSI, spectral/LPP, and the relevant Kusner et al. 2015 KNN-graph OT method) across multiple standard datasets and uses consistent clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure, etc.). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics, hyperparameters reported, code released) and practical scalability concerns of competing methods are discussed. However, the summary does not indicate inclusion of some more recent embedding-based or OT-based competitors (e.g., transformer-derived document embeddings or newer scalable OT approximations), and it is only partially clear whether cases where baselines match or beat the method are fully acknowledged and analyzed, so I deduct one point.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper adapts existing optimal-transport and D2-clustering ideas to word-embedding–based documents and contributes practical algorithmic and engineering improvements (modified B-ADMM barycenter solver, Elkan-style pruning, parallelization) that make OT-based clustering feasible on NLP datasets. The work is a meaningful and non-trivial applied/engineering advance with useful empirical validation and code release, but it does not introduce fundamentally new theoretical constructs or provable guarantees. Given this balance, a moderate originality score of 3/5 reflects the paper’s solid practical novelty without claiming major theoretical originality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies an existing conceptual framework (representing documents as discrete distributions and using 2nd-order Wasserstein distance) to word-embedding–based documents and implements D2-clustering with practical algorithmic improvements (modified B-ADMM for barycenters, parallelization and Elkan pruning). These elements are meaningful extensions that improve scalability and empirical performance over baselines (including Kusner et al., 2015), but they largely combine known optimal-transport ideas and engineering/implementation tricks rather than introducing fundamentally new theoretical constructs or provable guarantees. From a theory-oriented perspective, the contribution is a moderate, non-trivial methodological/engineering advance rather than a highly original theoretical breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper does not invent optimal transport or D2-clustering from scratch but presents a notable, well-differentiated application: representing documents as discrete distributions over word-embedding supports and adapting D2-clustering (with a modified B-ADMM barycenter solver, Elkan-style pruning, and parallel/vectorized implementation) to make Wasserstein-distance clustering practical for real NLP datasets. This combination of modern embeddings with scalable OT-based clustering and concrete engineering to address prior computational barriers is a non-trivial extension beyond prior KNN-graph and pairwise-distance approaches (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015). Given the code release and clear comparison across many standard corpora, the contribution would be informative to practitioners and shows notable novelty, though it is primarily an applied/engineering advance rather than a fundamentally new theory.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful practical contribution by making OT-based document clustering computationally viable and showing consistent empirical gains across standard datasets, with released code aiding adoption. However, they also agree its impact is limited by lack of theoretical guarantees and significant computational costs. Given its strong engineering value and likelihood to influence applied work despite limited theoretical novelty, I assign a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—document clustering with word‑embedding distributions using Wasserstein distances—and shows consistent empirical gains across multiple standard datasets (BBCNews, 20Newsgroups, Reuters, Ohsumed) and many baselines, with code released. However, from a theoretical/algorithmic perspective the contribution appears to be primarily engineering and empirical (modified B‑ADMM, parallelization, Elkan pruning) without accompanying convergence proofs, complexity bounds, or stability guarantees for the core optimization; that limits how strongly it will change researchers’ theoretical view. The approach does open useful applied directions (practical OT for NLP) and will likely be built upon by practitioners, but its impact is moderate rather than transformative because of the lack of formal guarantees and reliance on heavy computation/engineering.

reviewer_b.justification: This work addresses an important practical problem—accurate, robust document clustering—by combining word embeddings with discrete Wasserstein-distance clustering (D2-clustering) and demonstrating consistent AMI/ARI gains across multiple standard datasets (BBCNews, 20Newsgroups, Reuters, Ohsumed, etc.). The paper’s emphasis on making OT-based clustering computationally practical (modified B-ADMM, Elkan pruning, parallel/vectorized updates) plus released code increases likelihood practitioners will adopt and build on it. While computational cost and domain-specific embedding needs limit immediate universality, the empirical improvements and engineering contributions open viable directions for applied research and productionization of OT-based NLP methods.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is empirically strong, reproducible, and compares broadly against solid baselines across multiple datasets and metrics, but lacks formal theoretical guarantees and thorough component-level ablations. Given the clear empirical contribution, released code, and reproducibility while acknowledging the missing targeted analyses and formal bounds, a score of 4 reflects a solid contribution with room for deeper analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: Empirically the paper is reasonably comprehensive: multiple standard datasets (BBCNews, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, Ohsumed, etc.), many clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure) and comparisons to a wide set of baselines are reported, code and hyperparameters are provided so reproduction looks feasible. However, from a theoretical/algorithmic perspective it lacks provable guarantees or complexity/stability bounds for the modified B‑ADMM clustering procedure, and the summary does not indicate rigorous ablation or sensitivity studies isolating the impact of key components (b‑ADMM modifications, Elkan pruning, parallelization). The authors do discuss some limitations (computational intensity, domain-specific embedding needs) and related work is adequately covered, but the missing formal analysis and limited component-wise experiments prevent a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents broad empirical support across six diverse datasets (BBCNews, Wiki events, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, BBCSports, Ohsumed), compares to a wide set of strong baselines (tf-idf, PV, LDA, NMF, LSI, spectral methods, Kusner et al.), and reports multiple cluster metrics (AMI, ARI, v-measure) with code and hyperparameters released — all of which align with my emphasis on empirical effectiveness and reproducibility. It also documents practical engineering (modified B-ADMM, parallel/vectorized updates, Elkan pruning) and discusses computational limitations and dataset-specific issues (Ohsumed), supporting the scalability claims. However, I see only limited evidence of targeted ablations or sensitivity analyses isolating the contributions of algorithmic/implementation components (e.g., effects of pruning, B-ADMM variants, or parallelization) even though the paper asserts stability across document lengths, so some important analyses are missing. Related work coverage and negative-result discussion are adequate, so overall the work is solid with minor gaps in component-level ablation.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evidence is strong, the application of Wasserstein-based D2-clustering is plausible, and practical engineering makes the approach usable; they also both note missing theoretical convergence guarantees and limited statistical rigor/reporting. Given that the main claims are chiefly empirical and are supported by multiple datasets, released code, and sensible methodology, but with legitimate concerns about formal guarantees and statistical testing, a score of 4 reflects solid technical soundness with room for improvement in theory and evaluation rigor.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides extensive empirical comparisons across many datasets and baselines and reports clear gains using D2-clustering with Wasserstein distances and practical algorithmic engineering (modified B-ADMM, Elkan pruning). However, from the summary there is no mention of provable convergence or complexity bounds for the modified B-ADMM scheme or formal stability guarantees — a serious omission given my preference for theoretical guarantees for core optimization procedures. The experimental methodology includes many baselines and metrics but the summary does not report statistical significance testing, multiple-run error bars, or ablation isolating algorithmic components, so empirical claims of robustness/scalability are not fully substantiated. Overall the results look promising but technical gaps in theoretical analysis and rigorous statistical evaluation leave some of the main claims insufficiently supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims—that D2-clustering with Wasserstein distance over embedding-based discrete document distributions improves clustering and can be made practical via modified B-ADMM and engineering tricks—are well supported by experiments across multiple standard datasets, released code, and reported hyperparameters. Mathematical machinery (Wasserstein distance, barycenter computation) is plausibly applied and the implementation-level scalability measures are described, but the summary notes significant computational cost and only limited experiments on domain-specific data (Ohsumed), and it is unclear whether statistical significance tests were applied. Given these minor issues around runtime/scalability evidence and limited domain-specific validation, the technical claims are credible but not perfectly exhaustive, so I rate it 4/5.

main_claims: D2-clustering using Wasserstein distance over word-embedding-based discrete document distributions yields consistent and robust improvements over several existing document-clustering methods.

By leveraging recent algorithmic advances (modified B-ADMM) and implementation/parallelization tricks (e.g., Elkan pruning), the proposed approach is computationally practical and scalable for real datasets.
The nonparametric framework benefits from both bag-of-words and word embeddings and is stable across varying document lengths and task difficulties (achieving high AMI across datasets).
_summary.methodology: Represent each document as a discrete distribution over word-embedding support points and perform K-means–style clustering (D2-clustering) using 2nd-order Wasserstein distance; compute cluster barycenters via a modified Bregman ADMM algorithm with parallelized/vectorized updates and pruning (Elkan) in assignment steps.
limitations: Original D2-clustering required large-scale LPs and was computationally heavy; even with improvements, computing Wasserstein distances/barycenters is computationally intensive and requires engineering (parallelization/pruning).
Experiments on the Ohsumed dataset were limited and suggest domain-specific embeddings may be needed for best performance.
The Kusner et al. (2015) KNN-graph based approach remains limited in scalability due to needing many pairwise distance computations and curse of dimensionality.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - document clustering using word embeddings and optimal transport (Wasserstein distance)

reviewer_a.name: Mathematical Theorist
reviewer_a.background: Professor in machine learning theory with a focus on provable optimization and statistical guarantees for NLP models.
traits: Penalises submissions that make strong empirical claims without formal guarantees: deducts points if no convergence proof or formal complexity bound is provided for the core optimization procedure.

Requires new algorithmic/analytical insights beyond engineering heuristics: rewards formal novelty (new theorems, proofs, or provable improvements) and downranks primarily implementation/engineering contributions.
Demands statistically rigorous empirical evaluation: penalises if experiments lack significance testing, ablation studies isolating each component, or clear error bars across multiple runs.
Prefers worst-case complexity and stability analyses: deducts points when scalability claims are demonstrated only empirically without accompanying complexity/communication/memory analysis.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on the presence and quality of formal results and principled analysis; empirical results are secondary and must be supported by theory or rigorous statistical evaluation.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in applied NLP and large-scale ML systems, with experience shipping clustering and search components in production NLP pipelines.
traits: Rewards strong empirical improvements on realistic datasets: awards points when a method shows consistent gains across multiple, diverse benchmarks and metrics (e.g., AMI improvements on text datasets).

Values practical scalability and engineering contributions: increases score if the paper provides wall-clock runtime, memory usage, parallelization details, and demonstrates feasibility on real dataset sizes.

Credits methods that effectively combine modern representations with classical frameworks: favours principled use of word embeddings or optimal transport to improve downstream tasks even if theoretical guarantees are limited.
Likes reproducibility and usable artifacts: gives bonus credit if code, implementation details, and hyperparameters are released or sufficiently documented to reproduce experiments.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritise empirical effectiveness, scalability, and practical reproducibility; novel engineering and solid experimental validation often outweigh lack of deep formal theory.
